content.full_text:
From Jim Jeffery                                    30. October 1992

Could you please advise me of your position

on this matter vis-a-vis the attached letter.

If you need to get in touch with me please

call me at (408) 425-0426

Sincerely


James C. Jeffery III

[This appears to be a mostly blank page with very faint text at the top that is too illegible to accurately transcribe]

LAW OFFICES

ATCHISON, ANDERSON, HURLEY & BARISONE
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

RODNEY R. ATCHISON
NEAL R. ANDERSON (1947-1986)
JOHN G. BARISONE
VINCENT P. HURLEY
MITCHELL A. JACKMAN
JUSTIN B. LIGHTY
BRENDA J. FITZJOHN
ANN L. BUTLER
DAVID Y. IMAI

333 CHURCH STREET
SANTA CRUZ, CALIFORNIA 95060
TELEPHONE: (408) 423-8343
FAX: (408) 423-9401

6 EAST ALISAL
SUITE 601
SALINAS, CALIFORNIA 93901
(408) 753-1024
FAX: (408) 753-0293

October 19, 1992

James C. Jeffery III
ENGINEERING CONSULTING SERVICES
P.O. Box 1492
Santa Cruz, California 95061

RE: Conflict of Interest - City Contractors

Dear Mr. Jeffery:

This letter will respond to your October 9, 1992 letter. In that letter, you referenced an earlier telephone conversation in which, according to the letter, I advised you that because you sat on the City's Board of Building Appeals, conflict of interest regulations would preclude you from performing contract work for the City of Santa Cruz. This is not my recollection of the conversation. I recall you asking whether or not you could represent business clients before City boards and commissions while holding a seat on the Board of Building Appeals. The answer to the latter question is, in most circumstances, no. The answer to the former question is that a person who performs contract work directly for the City, by virtue of that fact alone, does not have a conflict of interest if he or she sits on a City board or commission. In fact, a number of City employees have sat on various City boards and commissions in the past.

For your future reference, I will summarize the applicable law for professional consultants such as yourself.

Government Code §87100 provides that no public official at any level of local government shall make or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence governmental decisions in which he has a financial interest. Pursuant to Government Code §87103, the official has a financial interest in a governmental decision when it is reasonably foreseeable that the government decision will have a material financial impact upon him or a member of his immediate family. Among other things, a decision is considered to have a material financial effect when that decision has the potential to result in income of $250.00 or more to the public official. 2 California Administrative Code §18702.1. An official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official appears before any officer of that agency. "Attempts to influence" include appearances by the official on behalf of a

ATCHISON, ANDERSON, HURLEY & BARISONE
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION


James C. Jeffery III
Page 2

client. 2 California Administrative Code §18700.1.

     An official is not attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if the official
appears in the same manner as any other member of the general
public before an agency solely to represent himself on a matter
which is related to his personal interests. Secondly, an official
is not attempting to use his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision if the official prepares drawings or
submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature
to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding before the
agency. However, this exception applies only if the official has
no other direct oral or written contact with the agency regarding
the client's project except for necessary contact with agency staff
concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions prepared by the official. Thirdly, an official is not
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental position if the official appears before a design or
architectural review committee or similar body of which he or she
is a member to present drawings or submissions of an architectural,
engineering or similar nature which the official has prepared for
a client if: (1) the review committee's sole function is to review
architectural or engineering plans or designs and to make
recommendations concerning those plans or designs to Planning
Commission or other agency; and (2) the ordinance or other
provision of law pursuant to which the review committee is
established requires the review committee to include architects,
engineers or persons in related professions, and the official was
appointed to the body to fulfill this requirement; and (3) the
official is a sole practitioner.

     With regard to the first referenced exception, you could
represent yourself before the Board of Building Appeals in a matter
related to property you own. With regard to the second exception,
you would be authorized to prepare drawings or submissions of an
engineering nature to be used by your client in connection with a
proceeding before the Board of Building Appeals. However, you
could have no other direct oral or written contact with the Board
pertaining to the client's project. Finally, the third exception
does not apply. If you are a sole practitioner, this exception
would only apply if the Board of Building Appeal's sole function
was to review architectural or engineering plans or designs and to
make recommendations with regard thereto and, secondly, only if the
ordinance or other provision of law establishing the Board of
Building Appeals required an engineer to sit thereon and you were
appointed pursuant to that provision. A review of the ordinance
code and the Zoning Board bylaws reveal that while the latter
requirement can be met (Chapter 18.41 of the Municipal Code
requires engineering professionals to sit on the Board), the former
cannot. In light of the foregoing, as a member of the Board of
Building Appeals, you could not appear before that body on behalf

ATCHISON, ANDERSON, HURLEY & BARISONE
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

James C. Jeffery III
Page 3

of your clients.

The next issue is whether this prohibition would likewise
forbid you from appearing before the City Council, Planning
Commission or Zoning Board on behalf of your clients in an effort
to obtain approval of projects for which you provided engineering
services.

We have been advised by a staff attorney for the Fair
Political Practices Committee that inasmuch as Board and Commission
members such as yourself were appointed to these positions by the
City Council, they are precluded from appearing before the City
Council, Planning Commission or Zoning Board in an effort to
influence decisions on projects in which they have a material
financial interest.  The issue here is whether, when appearing
before the City Council, Planning Commission or Zoning Board, you
would be appearing before your "agency".  The Fair Political
Practices Commission takes the position that the local government
agency in this case is the City of Santa Cruz. As a member of the
City's Board of Building Appeals, you are considered an official of
the City of Santa Cruz. On the basis of this legal construction,
the Fair Political Practices Commission advises that, in addition
to being precluded from appearing before the Board of Building
Appeals in an attmpt to influence governmental decisions in which
you have a material financial interest, you are similarly precluded
from appearing before the City Council, Zoning Board or Planning
Commission. In researching this area of law, I have come across no
case law or legislative history which would lead me to believe that
the Fair Political Practices Commission has erroneously come to
this conclusion. Accordingly, I must agree with the Fair Political
Practices Commission's interpretation.

In summary, our analysis of the applicable provisions of the
California Government Code and California Administrative Code leads
me to conclude that you may not appear before the Zoning Board,
Planning Commission or City Council on behalf of your clients with
regard to your clients' projects.

Very truly yours,

JOHN G. BARISONE

JGB:ln

sections.question_synthetic: A lawyer is responding to a client's inquiry about conflict of interest rules governing a city board member who provides engineering consulting services to clients with business before the city.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This appears to be incoming correspondence (a lawyer's advisory letter to a client) rather than an FPPC response letter. The document references advice from FPPC staff but is not written by FPPC. No formal question/conclusion sections. The document contains substantive legal analysis and exceptions related to Government Code §87100 and §87103. Confidence is moderate due to unclear document provenance - it's a law firm letter discussing FPPC positions but not an official FPPC advice letter.

embedding.summary: This is a lawyer's letter (Atchison, Anderson, Hurley & Barisone) responding to James C. Jeffery III, a member of Santa Cruz's Board of Building Appeals and engineering consultant, clarifying conflict of interest rules under California Government Code. The lawyer advises that Jeffery cannot appear before the Board of Building Appeals, City Council, Planning Commission, or Zoning Board on behalf of clients regarding projects where he has a material financial interest, except in limited circumstances.
